title: 2026 Season Overview: Two Rounds In
meta: Published: 20 March 2026 | By Camille Dubois, Opinion Columnist | F1CC News
caption: 2026 season overview.
body:
Ferrari are running away with it.

That’s not a prediction. That’s what two races of the 2026 season have told us. One chaotic wet afternoon in Melbourne. One tactically intricate sprint weekend under the Shanghai sun. Two completely different tests. One team solved both. The rest of the grid is scrambling for answers, and some of them haven’t even figured out the question.

The Ferrari Picture

Here’s the thing: the wins are impressive, but it’s the variety that should terrify the paddock. Melbourne was a full‑wet survival exercise, reshuffled by punctures and safety cars, where luck should have played a bigger hand than it did. Shanghai was dry, strategic, built entirely on tyre offsets and pit‑wall timing. Two opposite challenges, one identical result. Väisänen didn’t just win those races; he controlled them. And the team behind him made zero mistakes on the pit wall.

That’s the good news for Ferrari. Now the bad news: Afonso Cachinho. The heavily scrutinised number two has not scored a single point. He has been penalised in both sessions of the Chinese weekend, sits on six penalty points already, and has a formal warning from the stewards on his record. A driver who was handed a seat that scored 226 points last season is giving Ferrari nothing. If they want to hold that constructor lead, they need a clean weekend from him. Desperately. Two Q1 exits and a late‑race collision that cost him five seconds and a points finish is not that.

The Challengers and Japan

Quarter Kevu is the most credible threat to Väisänen, and the paddock knows it. The reigning champion hasn’t been dominant, but he’s been relentlessly consistent. That’s how championships are built when the leader eventually stumbles. Jason Roy and Red Bull, by contrast, are the most volatile element in the standings: massive raw pace, pole positions, and a race‑day execution that keeps handing races to Ferrari. Roy’s anger after China wasn’t just frustration; it was a warning that Red Bull are not far away, but they’re making the wrong calls at the wrong moments.

Mercedes came into 2026 with optimism, and Vandesteene’s P2 in Melbourne felt like confirmation that the team had arrived. Then he locked up on lap one in Shanghai and retired. That’s the gap between a contender and a champion: consistency across two weekends. Mercedes have one brilliant result and one disaster. That won’t win a championship.

Suzuka is next. A driver’s circuit in the truest sense: high‑speed, unforgiving, and technically demanding in a way that numbers on a screen don’t capture. Ferrari have the aero efficiency and the driver to win there. Red Bull have shown they can qualify on the front row anywhere. But qualifying is not racing. The question that should keep everyone else awake: how long can Ferrari sustain this? Two races in, the answer is already worrying. They haven’t put a foot wrong. Cachinho’s struggles aside, the scarlet machine is operating at a level that no one else is matching. The core ingredients for a season‑long rout are perfectly in place.

As the season grinds on, reliability and pit‑wall execution will become just as vital as qualifying speed. Every point is fiercely contested now, and the margins for error have vanished. The teams that manage internal pressure while pushing development will be the ones standing on the top step when this is over. But right now, only one team looks capable of that. The rest are just making up the numbers. So here’s the question you might be afraid to answer: is this championship already over?
